evidence: Both benchmark AI agents on scientific discovery tasks; FIRE-Bench evaluates full-cycle insight rediscovery while SciSciGPT focuses on paper reproduction.
ScienceAgentBench evaluates LLM agents on data-driven scientific tasks with 102 tasks from 44 papers; both share the goal of rigorous agent evaluation.
CORE-Bench evaluates computational reproducibility of published papers; SciSciGPT automates paper reproduction. Both address reproducibility assessment.
FIRE-Bench extends ScienceAgentBench's task-based evaluation to full-cycle scientific rediscovery, adding experimental design and conclusion derivation.
ReplicatorBench extends CORE-Bench's reproducibility focus to social/behavioral sciences, adding non-replicable claims and data retrieval challenges.
DiscoveryBench evaluates hypothesis search and verification from data; ScienceAgentBench evaluates task-level data-driven scientific workflows. Complementary benchmarks.
AAAR-1.0 evaluates LLMs on expert research tasks (review, experiment design); SciReplicateBench evaluates algorithmic reproduction. Both assess AI research assistance.
Part 1 establishes direct reproducibility taxonomy; Part 2 extends to conceptual reproducibility. Two-part framework covering the full reproducibility spectrum.
Waltman et al.'s reproducibility taxonomy (2018) provides theoretical foundation for CORE-Bench's computational reproducibility evaluation framework.
ProjectionBench evaluates hypothesis generation under progressive disclosure; FIRE-Bench evaluates full discovery cycle. Both use automated semantic similarity for evaluation.
SRI's per-cell reproducibility metric extends Kapoor's audit framing to component-level measurement (axis b prior art).
Theiler's paper-to-benchmark agent extends ScienceAgentBench's paper-derived tasks toward full-paper reproduction (axis a prior art).
Liu's measurement/methods primer extends the foundational SciSci agenda with a measurement-standard perspective.
